sentences.text: Well, everything is relative.
There was-- we came with the Schindler's group from Gross Rosen, which was the most brutal camp where people were starving to death.
They were fighting for a-- crumbs-- for bread crumbs.
There was a-- pov-- there was the worst condition I ever saw.
And there, they were beating people to death.
They were-- everything-- the most possible torture-- just-- just-- the most vicious treatment that anybody could-- could imagine.
Oh, we were fed, I think, a-- I don't know-- a piece of bread and some watery kind of soup once a day.
And in the morning, some kind of a lotion-- I mean, some-- some kind of a liquid that resembled-- I don't know-- black something.
It wasn't coffee, for sure, because coffee was not available anyway.
And that was unimaginable.
Oh, yes, we have a striped uniform.
Shaved heads.
Striped uniforms with the number.
No, we had it all the time.
Oh, in Plaszow we had uniforms.
In Gross Rosen we had uniforms.
And at Schindler's we had uniforms.
Always striped uniforms.
Vertical stripes.
And th-- and hats, too.
Well, hats were different.
Some-- some people had--
Clogs.
Clogs.
Clogs.
Not shoes.
Not shoes.
Clogs with leather-- with leather-- they were not soles.
They were clogs.
No socks.
We  had some rags around our feet.
There were no socks.
In-- at--
At Gross Rosen?
Well, the first night-- I think it's described in a few books and everywhere was such an unimaginable nightmare that nobody-- it's hard to explain.
It's hard to believe.
OK, to give you an idea.
We were put, 2,000-- all right-- 2,000 men in one room, which was like a little-- like a little ex-- well, you would compare it today to a little exercise room, maybe.
So now you figure 2,000 men, and we were supposed to spend a night there.
So what an ingenious-- how ingenious the German were, what they did.
Now, I don't have to tell you about the sanitary condition.
It got to be, somebody wanted to get up, if he got up, that was the end of it.
He could never get back.
That's how we spent  the whole night.
It was most nightmarish that anybody can imagine.
That was in--
October.
Well, inside, that particular night, that wasn't so cold.
But prior to that, what we had to go through-- well, that was an interesting experience.
They-- they brought us to-- in front of a barrack, and they made us undress completely.
And mind you, it was, like, the end of October.
It was very cold.
This was a-- And then they send us to a shower, which was a very-- it was a cold shower.
And then they had some kind of a disinfectant.
But it was so crude.
There was a barrel with this disinfectant.
It was very crude, very sharp.
And they had, on a wooden stick, it was like a super-giant swab, you can call it.
And they-- they-- they-- they strike-- struck everybody with that swab, in the front and the back.
But before this, they had-- they checked everybody.
They made us bend down to look if there's any hidden diamonds or any valuables there.
Then, after that cold shower, we were wet.
There was nothing to-- to dry out.
They chased us when we were-- they figured out-- it was, like, over a mile to some barracks.
But that was the second night.
That's where we sort of slept two in a bunker.
It was also just plain bunk-- plain bunker--
That was in Gross Rosen, yeah.
About seven, eight days.
We stayed, the people who went to Brunnlitz.
Some people stayed there for about two weeks.
This friend of mine stayed for two weeks.
He told me about the other people.
Oh, it was completely different.
First, what they-- they brought us there.
So even the guards who were there-- they were a little bit older.
They didn't look so-- as mean as the ones that we saw before, who were mostly young.
I would say in their mid-20s-- early 20s, mid-20s.
These are-- these were the most vicious.
The ones who were in-- in Brunnlitz, I would say they were maybe, I don't know, 40-ish or so.
And, like I said, they didn't have that-- that-- that-- that mean-- that vicious look in their eyes.
And then the whole life was changed already there.
There was such a contrast, after Gross Rosen, there, so--

Well, it was changed that, as they unloaded us from the train, right, and they took us to the factory which was, by the way-- there was-- the whole camp was an abandoned textile mill which belonged to some-- I don't know-- to some Czechs before.
It was completely abandoned.
And then we were supposed to make ourself-- to stay there.
So we had to build the bunk-- build the-- the-- the bunk beds and to start from scratch everything.
Then they were starting putting-- preparation for putting the machinery down in that-- in the hall-- in the work-- the factory itself.
So we had to remove, first, this old textile machinery, which was-- which was worthless anyway.
It was-- it was-- It wasn't working.
And then they had to dig and to put-- set up foundations, cement for the heavy presses.
And we have sort of like a choice as to the work here.
Then, of course, the-- the rule number one was to build a fence-- electrifying fence.
And my friend and I, we had experience.
We realized that If you work outdoor, even though it's cold, but you're sort of more close to the world.
I don't know.
Maybe in our-- in our fantasy we were thinking we are closer to-- maybe not to freedom, but somehow you feel better if you're outdoors.
Anyway.
So it was easy for us to-- to get on that group, because it was very cold.
By then it was-- it was getting cold.
It was, like, November already, December.
It was quite cold.
But we still realized that we'd-- we'd be better off.
So we start.
We worked on the detail to build fences around that camp, which had to-- had to be electrified.
But of course we never got around to electrifying them, because the war ended.
So it shows you, we took, like, four-- four months just to build those fences.
I mean, there was no particular hurry to do this.
But there was some-- we used to start fires for the-- for the-- for the SS guards for this.
So we had an idea.
You see, even though it was [INAUDIBLE] idea, we had an idea that we built a little hole-- hole and cover it up with a tin and build a fire on top of it for them.
But in the meantime, while we go, there were some fields, so we would dig out some beets-- red beets-- and sometimes found potatoes.
So we would throw the potatoes to that hole, cover up with the tin, and-- and made a fire, and start a fire on top of it.
Then when it came to, I think, 12:00, 1:00, [INAUDIBLE] they brought us some soup.
There was a-- I don't know, we had, like, a half hour or so lunch break.
So we would go into that fire and-- and grab our-- and take out those potatoes and beets.
Sometimes we were caught by them.
Sometimes, I don't know, depends on their mood, they would take it and kick all those potatoes and beets away from us.
And sometimes they'd just turn around-- didn't pay attention.
So they didn't care.
They-- they saw that the end was coming near.
They saw that.
Otherwise, the food was-- one day there was an interesting incident.
The comm-- one-- one-- one SS man comes over, and as a matter of fact, had the-- the the-- with the-- with his rifle slung over his shoulder.
And he says-- and he picks by hand, and he took us, I think, eight men.
And there was a truck standing by.
And-- and we couldn't get out of it.
We didn't know what he was doing.
But then we didn't think,  really, something really bad-- why would it-- why would they pick us, or pick us up?
And he drove us.
But then, he was the only one who sat down next to the driver.
And we were in the back of a truck.
And he drove us out of the camp, so we figured it cannot be that bad, because we could easily jump off the truck, because it was an open-- open truck.
But nobody dared, because we-- we realized that, really, nothing really bad could happen to us.
So they brought us to the village.
And there was an injured-- a truck had struck a horse, and the horse was laying there.
So one of them came over, and he shot the horse immediately, and then made us load up that horse on the truck.
We brought to the camp, and we had meat for-- for three days.
So-- so then we found out later on that Schindler found out about that horse, and he had the idea that we should bring the horse to camp so he could feed us.
And it was a little gesture on his part.
Oh, I saw him every day, practically-- practically every day.
Sure.
Yes.
Yes.
In May, 1945.
Well, I fell into it.
They-- they-- they didn't really--
I just-- because we were so-- so-- so proud.
I mean, we figured after all, what we've suffered, we're going now to our home town.
So we didn't try to avoid any authorities.
Just the opposite.
We thought that we are on the part of the victors, sort of.
So we just fell in, and there was no way out.
No way out.
In that camp?
Two weeks.
Those two weeks, they were like-- like-- like two years or more.
There was the biggest torture, because the torture was mental torture.
Well, first of all, you have to-- the food was given once a day.
What does mean, 'once a day?' At 7:00 o'clock in the morning, there were two-- two big kettles, and there were, like, two-- two Russians at each.
And they were dishing out bread and soup, from 7:00 in the morning till, I think, 9, 10:00 at night, continuously.
But there were a few thousand people.
So now you figure how long it takes to get.
So when they start, 7:00 in the morning, if you got on line, so maybe 11:00 you got that little soup and bread.
And if you want a second, or getting more, so you stand on line again.
So maybe 6:00 in the evening you got another portion.
That's why there was continu-- there was no-- no-- no mealtimes.
Mealtime was from 7:00 in the morning to, like I said, till-- till late in the evening.
Not quite, no.
No, no.
We still had our striped clothing from the-- from the camp.
No, we didn't have any regular clothing.
Yeah, they asked us.
Yeah, once I went to an officer and I told him, because I speak Russian.
He says, well, why didn't you go into the partisans and why didn't you fight with, like, our boys?
And that was it.
Then we tried, and they just-- they were completely oblivious to our-- they-- they didn't care.
They didn't care one way or another.
They were so bitter because every one of them-- I mean, the Russians, they lost millions and millions.
Everybody-- every one of them lost member of a family, a friend, or so far.
So they just-- they were very hardened.
Very hardened.
No.
No.
No.
No.
No.
We saw maybe two-- two or three.
And they-- they had the same problem that we have.
Nobody wanted to talk to them.
But we saw a few thousand of those-- of those who were shooting, killing us.
Well, I didn't see particularly anyone that I could recognize, that he did something to whom.
But they knew with whom they had the Russians.
No.
No, not then.
I did get back to Boryslaw in 1993, with my son, which is a year and a half ago.
Oh, we got-- with a group of Poles, we got on a train and we went west.
Then, subsequently, everybody went somewhere else, and this friend of mine and I, we went to Krakow.
And then there's a whole new life started.
Well, we met some people.
Matter of fact, one-- one girl who had an apartment in-- in Krakow.
No.
At that time, we abandoned those uniforms and we-- since, when we left Schindler's, each one was given some fabrics that they had in a warehouse.
I don't know, it was like a-- I think a blue fabric and a grey [INAUDIBLE].
So we traded in for some-- some old clothing.
So we didn't wear any-- no, not anymore.
As a matter of fact, when we-- oh, now I remember.
We traded in those fabrics in that camp-- in Russian camp-- for some old rags, because we were known already by-- by-- everybody knew us that saw us in those striped uniforms.
So we exchanged it there.
So then, when we went to reregister again with this group of Poles, we already had quote-unquote 'civilian clothes,' some old rags, and tried to change our appearance.
And then th-- this girl had a-- well, apartment.
It was, like, a one-room or two-room apartment.
In Krakow, yes.
And we came-- so they told us-- she happened to-- she was from our town.
And this friend of mine, we came over there, and there was-- unbelievable picture.
In their living room, bedroom, there were mattresses strung all over, from wall to wall.
And she told us that there are, like-- like 10 or 12 people.
Every-- whoever comes in stays there, sleeps there, and whatever meager food she had, she-- she-- she was feeding us.
So we stayed there for three, four days.
And then-- then continued, then.
I found out that one girl from our town had a sister who had-- who was married, at that time, to a very influential person in-- from Russia.
They came from Russia, and he became the head of the petroleum industry of the-- they call it-- [INAUDIBLE] in Poland.
So this friend of mine went over there, and she gave us a job as chauffeur.
She, since I learned how to work in an auto shop-- a repair shop-- I learned how to drive.
So she gave us a job so to-- to drive them, to be their personal chauffeur.
And there was an-- an old stable.
They give us a room there.
And so it was fine.
And we stayed there for about-- oh, for about four or five weeks.
And then I decided-- he fell in love with this girl, but that's a long story.
She-- she-- she-- she didn't want him, anyway, but he stayed there.
And I left for the West, which was at the American-occupied territory.
And I subsequently wound up in Salzburg, in Austria.
I was supposed to stay there for one day and then go, the following day, to-- to Munich, with a-- with a transport of Jews.
No, nobody sent-- no, no, no.
When we left Poland--
Not quite.
Yeah, there was a camp, but-- but that wasn't like this.
When we left Po-- when we-- as I say, I left Poland with some other people, but not this particular friend who's been with me all the time.
We-- we-- we-- we ran out of different papers.

We had to reregister that we are-- some of them registered as Greek, as Czech-- anything but-- so we was going to-- to be with-- We want to go to our homeland, which, obviously, in order to go to Greece from Poland, you have to go west and south, et cetera.
So they let us out, the Russians.
But they didn't care so much. the Polish they just-- they didn't care.
So finally we wound up in Linz, which is northern-- all through Germany, it was a whole odyssey.
We-- we hitched-- hitchhiked trains and-- and with people, and we stayed over in farmhouses, and they fed us and gave us food, et cetera.
And then we wound up in-- in Linz, which is the northern part of Austria.
And subsequently we had to go through Salzburg.
Salzburg is on the border of Austria and Germany.
It's about, like, four miles from this-- from the heart of the town-- from-- from-- from downtown Salzburg to the German border.
So we were supposed to stay there the night, the next day go to-- to Germany.
There was a camp-- a big Jewish camp.
And from there, there were transports going to Israel, and somebody want to stay in Germany, at DP camp.
So we-- I went to-- to Salzburg.
I stayed overnight.
And the next day was a beautiful day, so I figured, ah, let me stay another day.
Why not?
And I stayed for four years, there, really, because I liked the town.
So I never went anywhere else.
What was the name of that DP camp?
No, there were-- which one, in Salzburg?
Well, there were a few.
I didn't stay there.
I stayed only a few days.
There was Parsch, P-A-R-S-C-H, was one.
And there was Camp Bialik, named after the Nahman Bialik, who was a-- activist and writer.
Well, actually, on and off.
Three years, and I was a-- a year in Vienna, when I went to the university, medical school, for a year.
About a year.
Well, not quite.
I had a sister who was attending school too, and so--
Oh, yes.
Oh, [INAUDIBLE], that's right.
I had met the sister in Krakow.
In 40-- yeah, the street.
On the street.
It wasn't--
On the-- just-- just-- just-- just out of nowhere.
There was a-- there was a-- there was a point where a lot of Jews were-- were congregating.
Everybody was trying to look who's-- who was alive, who was [INAUDIBLE].
And one day I'm walking and a friend of mine says, look who is there.
Of course, she had-- her head was shaven, but it was like a little bit of hair was growing.
Of course.
Well, I had only-- I hadn't seen her only six months, seven months.
So of course I--
Well, that's a story.
Her husband survived, but she didn't know that.
And then we found out him being with another woman.
So I arranged some kind of a divorce, which obviously, at that time, it's not valid, but was valid, sort of.
And then she remarried, subsequently, and I guess he remarried this woman, too.
19-- till I came to America. 19-- May, 1949.
No, the DP Act.
Well, I had a family, but there was part of that Truman Act, Displaced Person Acts, under which thousands of Jews and non-Jews came to the United States.
That's the refugee act.
Beside that, I was sponsored by my cousin.
But that was secondary.
Even people who didn't have actual sponsors by name, they came, too.
Just like my wife didn't have anybody.
I don't think she had anybody.
So that wasn't that-- very important, although I did have.
Oh, yes.
I lived-- I lived with them.
I lived with them for about-- about six weeks, two months.
I stayed with them.
Oh, yes, they were very cordial.
They were very nice to me.
They were very dear.
Yes.
They were very good to me.
They were nice.
To New York.
I stayed in Brooklyn with them, yes.
In New York.
Oh, I had a series of odd jobs.
I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I started off as a busboy-waiter in a Catskill hotel, this small, little Catskill hotel.
Then I worked for a machine shop.
They were repairing clutches because there's a certain-- there was something in me that had to do with automobiles.
And then, subsequently, as my knowledge of English language improved, I began working as a sort of salesman and for a company-- fur-processing company.
And then a friend of mine told me about a possible position of a sales-- in sales for a costume-jewelry factory.

And even though I was already making-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was-- I was earn-- my earnings, at that time, already were quite good, working for the fur-processing company, I left it and started working for less than half for that co-- costume-jewelry factory, because I liked it.
I began to like it, and I thought a better chance I had.
And-- And I stuck with that till-- in this-- in this industry-- till the end.
Well, I had a chance of getting help.
I didn't get any, because I didn't req-- I didn't ask them.
But those who didn't have anybody, who didn't have where to stay, they were getting help from-- from HIAS.
Well, HIAS is the organization that brought me over here.
They officially sponsored me, even though we came on a-- an American transport ship.
But I was offered shelter and possibly some food.
But I just didn't--
No, but she came about a month.
Within a month after me, she came, too.
That's correct.
That's correct.
That's right.
I had to get accustomed to the American life, because while I was four years in Salzburg, I got used to a certain-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] certain comfort.
I had a car there, and I had a cer-- certain level.
I established my life-- lifestyle there.
Here I came, I had to start from scratch.
I didn't mind, but sort of-- I-- I persevered.
And I adjusted quite well to the-- to the American life.
I had none.
I had none.
I-- I learned from the past, and this stays to this day.
I don't expect too much, or very little, and if you get more, then it's a bonus.
So this way, there was no disappointment for me, none whatsoever.
Yes, I married. '49, I came; I married [INAUDIBLE].
About five years after I came to America, I married.
Well, I met my wife on a double date.
She was with my best friend, and I was with another girl.
And then she-- she-- I think she saw him maybe once.
And then, about a year-- and then, of course, we-- I had no contact with her.
I just happened to meet her that double date.
And then, about a year later, I had met her.
And I knew that she has no contact with my friend, even though I'm still-- I was still with my friend all the time.
We were very-- on very good terms.
No, no, no.
This was a different friend.
No.
That's right.
No.
No, no.
This friend I know from my town.
And the bottom line is that I married her, and he married somebody else.
And we became-- we still maintain our friendship with him and his wife to this day.
He happens to be in Miami, live in the same building where I live, here.
